Hm, let me answer your question with a question:

- So how many 302/303s do you want to follow?

> in all cases, if there is any error, don't do a retry and force the "real" error to be raised

So by default, I think urllib3 will try a maximum of 10 (over all things it retries) retries. I think you can disable that elegantly by passing `retries=False` but I've forgotten and haven't used that in a while. I'll need a few minutes to confirm.

If you don't care about following a potentially large number of redirects, you should be able to just say `redirects=True` and be done with all of it.

My example above was allowing for exactly 1 redirect and otherwise returning the status code of the second response (in the event of a redirect) and raising errors otherwise.

Yes, I see now that the certfile and keyfile are not available on the context after the load call.  This might be because those parameters are allowed to be either file names _or file-like_.

Another idea might be to store the certfile and keyfile as additional attributes on the `config` object, and then make the `config` object available in the place where `clientFromString()` is called.  I _feel like_ there is a right solution here, but it probably involves some additional Twisted knowledge.  I'll defer to @nikipore here.  Clearly, it's the Twisted stuff that is doing extra things with the handling of the SSL connection, and if we must go via `clientFromString()` then we'll have to provide the certfile and keyfile into that command, as you've already done.

Yeah, py-zipkin actually leaves the header extraction to whatever's calling it, e.g. pyramid-zipkin. It just lets you pass in arbitrary headers, which it calls zipkin_attrs, then automatically joins the upstream client span (related to comment above about the outermost `zipkin_span`), always logging ('sr', 'ss').

The brave model is more expressive and complete, but this gets the job done for most sane use cases.

Let's change iteration order. Roughly
``` for k, v in self:
         if k in other and other[k] == v:
              ...
```
By this, we are independent of a mapping order.
Keep in mind: `other` can be not only `dict` instance but any dict-like object.
The check is a comparison to mapping, not to iterable of pairs.
The dict is not comparable with a list of pairs:
```
In [1]: {'a': 1} == [('a', 1)]
Out[1]: False
```
That's fine for multidict too.

For me, everything's fine without virtualenv, mostly because I've never had a package complain about a dependency being too *new*. So for me, it works well to install the pip version of a module if the one from the Ubuntu repository is too old, and then it automatically takes precedence over the repository version so I don't have to uninstall the latter (possibly leaving Ubuntu package dependencies unresolved).
For some modules like numpy and scipy, I do site-wise installs because on our GPU machines, all users (yes, all users, I've asked them) want the latest version, can't install it with `--user` (since home directories are shared between multiple machines that sometimes have different BLAS libraries to link against) and would be very annoyed if they had to learn virtualenv and create a different environment per server.
So for me, the only use case for virtualenv is for checking whether a particular piece of software I wrote can be deployed on machines running an older version of Python, numpy or scipy. But this is not one of my day-to-day activities.

Probably you had a different experience, but I'd still try not to scare people too much ("dependency hell" and "fucking up your computer" sounds scary, even if worded differently), just give them the option of using virtualenv and let them decide. It should be as easy as possible to get from zero to Lasagne.

> http://www.dabapps.com/blog/introduction-to-pip-and-virtualenv-python/

The reason for the different output is that `apache2ctl` additionally sources `/etc/conf.d/apache2` and then adds the `APACHE2_OPTS` defined there to the `apache2` call:

Example for `APACHE2_OPTS` in `/etc/conf.d/apache2`:
`APACHE2_OPTS="-D INFO -D LANGUAGE -D SSL -D PHP -D SECURITY -D PROXY"`

Function that gets executed when calling `apache2ctl virtualhosts`:
```
virtualhosts() {
        checkconfig || return 1
        ${APACHE2} ${APACHE2_OPTS} -S
}
```
The options in `APACHE2_OPTS` are used in Gentoo to define includes in vhosts, like in `/etc/apache2/vhosts.d/00_default_ssl_vhost.conf`:
```
<IfDefine SSL>
<IfDefine SSL_DEFAULT_VHOST>
<IfModule ssl_module>

# see bug #178966 why this is in here

# When we also provide SSL we have to listen to the HTTPS port
# Note: Configurations that use IPv6 but not IPv4-mapped addresses need two
# Listen directives: "Listen [::]:443" and "Listen 0.0.0.0:443"
Listen 443

<VirtualHost _default_:443>
        ...
</VirtualHost>
</IfModule>
</IfDefine>
</IfDefine>
```
For me, it worked perfectly without having the flags included but there may be indeed some side effects. But while using `apache2ctl` renders certbot completely useless for Gentoo systemd users (automatic installation AND certonly are broken), using `apache2` "only" breaks the automatic installation, leaving workarounds using certonly functional.

To fix this issue completely, certbot requires support for different commands depending on the init system. Until that is done I would say generally enabling all users to use certonly is better then breaking the support for one group (especially since systemd seems to be becoming industry standard) while enabling full support for the other.
